weather: Today in Edmonds: Cloudy morning giving way to partial afternoon clearing. High 57°F, low 46°F. South winds 5-10 mph. Only 20% chance of light rain, mainly staying dry. Evening turns partly cloudy with light variable winds. Cool but comfortable spring day—light jacket recommended.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has wrapped auditions for 2025-26, but invites singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the August 2026 mailing list for the 2026-27 season. Auditions are held at Epiphany Parish (1805 38th Ave, Seattle) and require a 2-minute solo plus prepared excerpts; dues $75/year with scholarships. Seattle Choral Company will audition in September 2026 for its 2026-27 season—sign-up form is live at seattlechoruses.org/join-chorus; rehearsals are Tuesday evenings, dues $225. Northwest Chorale and Choral Arts NW have not yet posted 2026 audition dates; check their sites in late summer.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries schedule Toddler Storytime at both Edmonds and Lynnwood branches. Edmonds offers weekly 9:30 am sessions (doors open 9:15-9:35) with songs, movement, and post-story play. Lynnwood lists Toddler Storytime with Nathalie on Mondays 9:30–10:00 am. Both libraries also run Baby Storytime (newborn–18 mo) and Preschool Storytime (3–5 yrs); full May calendars are on sno-isle.bibliocommons.com.

intellectual_journals.findings: In 'Why You', Maria Popova explores the philosophical implications of identity and selfhood through the lens of cosmic contingency. The essay argues that our sense of self is fundamentally a selective narrative constructed from countless random events - from atomic bonds to parental relationships to cultural values. Drawing on Iris Murdoch's observation that 'the self, the place where we live, is a place of illusion,' Popova suggests that true goodness involves piercing this veil of selfish consciousness to connect with reality as it truly is. The piece weaves together insights from Eastern ph [TRUNCATED]

In 'Movements Need the Critical Thinking That AI Destroys', Jacobin presents a philosophical critique of AI's impact on human subjectivity and democratic participation. The article argues that while AI systems provide efficiency gains, they create what the author terms 'cognitive debt' - the gradual erosion of our capacity for independent thought through the outsourcing of judgment to algorithms. Drawing on a recent MIT study showing reduced brain activity among regular chatbot users, the piece frames AI as creating a 'decaffeinated subjectivity' that threatens democratic processes and emancip [TRUNCATED]

intellectual_journals.findings: In 'Waiting for Day Zero', Will Alden provides an in-depth portrait of Iranian opposition groups in Los Angeles as they navigate the current US-Iran conflict. The piece follows the Constitutionalist Party of Iran (CPI) during their Easter Sunday picnic at Lake Balboa Park, where supporters of former crown prince Reza Pahlavi gather with prerevolutionary flags and chants of 'Long Live the Shah.' The essay reveals how these exile communities have become politically significant in Republican circles, with weekly protests at Westwood's Wilshire Federal Building drawing thousands. The 'Tehrangeles' [TRUNCATED]

In 'Mass Arrests by Trump's ICE, CBP Repeatedly Fail Under Scrutiny', ProPublica and FRONTLINE investigate the systematic failure of federal immigration enforcement cases against protesters. The investigation documents over 300 arrests of U.S. citizens - including Marine veteran Alejandro Orellana - accused of crimes like assaulting law enforcement during immigration protests. Through extensive review of court records and video footage, the piece reveals how these cases repeatedly collapse under scrutiny, with officers' statements debunked by video evidence and charges dismissed due to lack of [TRUNCATED]

The device offers voice-powered Cosmos OS for instant AI knowledge. HP is actively hiring for next-gen AI wearables through HP IQ. Building Humane Technology community (buildinghumanetech.com) focuses on ethical AI device design with  [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: The Triadic Closure of Existence: Co-Emergence of Binding, Transformation, and Relational Readout (15 Apr 2026, Zenodo) argues that reality is not built additively but through a simultaneous, phase-locked triadic closure: three mutually necessary functional roles—stable binding, transformative change, and relational legibility—must co-emerge for any observable universe to arise. Author shows that the three fundamental physical interaction classes map exactly onto these roles, turning “three forces” from a numerical curiosity into a structural imperative. A second, complementary paper, A Triadic Ontology with SU(3) Invariants (Trent Slade, Zenodo 2025), constructs a minimal ontic kernel using qutrit-based dynamics: states are density operators on a 3-D Hilbert space, dynamics are SU(3) actions, and closure is enforced by a ℤ₃ rotation operator that prevents collapse of the third state. The resulting 3-8-2 schema (three basis stances, eight generators, two conserved classifiers) is prese [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The field has moved decisively from single-agent copilots to orchestrated, multi-agent research pipelines. Google Cloud’s PaperOrchestra (April 2026) demonstrates the new ceiling: five specialized agents—literature reviewer, outline generator, figure creator, citation verifier and LaTeX formatter—collaborate to turn raw lab notes into conference-ready manuscripts, executing ~4 k LLM calls per paper and outperforming earlier end-to-end systems that were tightly coupled to their own experiments. The Auton framework (arXiv 2602.23720) formalizes the architecture shift, separating a declarative “Cognitive Blueprint” from a deterministic Runtime Engine so agents can be audited, ported and governed across clouds. In astrophysics, the Cmbagent swarm used by Cambridge and Berkeley achieved first place in the FAIR Universe Weak Lensing Challenge by letting human experts intervene when autonomous exploration stalled, proving that semi-autonomous loops now rival domain specialists. Prompt enginee [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The New Yorker’s Talk of the Town: April 28, 2026
newyorker.dek: At a pop-up dinner in the Flatiron District, a chef serves a twenty-course tasting menu that doubles as a performance-art piece about climate change.
newyorker.text: On a recent Tuesday evening, in a former textile warehouse on West Twenty-sixth Street, the chef and artist Laila Gohar staged what she called a “culinary elegy” for the planet. Guests—who had paid seven hundred and fifty dollars each—were greeted by a host wearing a dress made of dried kelp and escorted past a wall of slowly melting ice sculptures shaped like endangered animals. The meal began with a single clam, served on a rock that had been flown in from the coast of Maine, followed by a succession of increasingly theatrical dishes: a broth made from the bones of invasive lionfish, served in bowls that cracked open to reveal edible flowers; a terrarium of microgreens grown under heat lamps that flickered like wildfire; and, for the final course, a chocolate truffle filled with carbon-dioxide foam, which dissolved on the tongue like a dying glacier. Gohar, who has previously collaborated with the artist Olafur Eliasson, told the crowd that the dinner was meant to evoke “the last supper of the Anthropocene.” Between courses, a string quartet played a mournful arrangement of “What a Wonderful World,” while a projection mapped rising sea levels onto the ceiling. Some guests wept; others took notes on their phones. At the end of the night, Gohar took a bow, her apron stained with squid ink and ash. “We wanted to make something beautiful,” she said, “before it all disappears.”
